notes: Eval 0 (bootstrap on a cross-compiled project) is the discriminating case: with_skill 5/5, baseline 1/5. The baseline invented a completely different mechanism (CMake git-describe + VERSION file) — no dependency snapshot, no target-mode record, nothing for future releases to diff against.

Evals 1 and 2 baselines pass 5/5 because the committed dependency.md itself documents the convention — the file format is self-teaching, which is by design. The skill's value concentrates in bootstrap and in edge-case correctness.

Hidden baseline deviation the loose assertions missed: baseline BUILD numbers used commits-since-tag (1.3.0+2.g..., 0.9.2+1.g...) instead of the spec's total commit count (with_skill: +3/+2). A stricter BUILD assertion would catch this in future iterations.
Skill runs are ~2x faster with far lower variance (111s ± 10 vs 198s ± 79): the skill eliminates mechanism-design detours. Token cost is +6.4k for reading the skill.
One baseline run (eval-1) crashed mid-output-save due to an API error; its outputs were salvaged from the repo state, grading unaffected.
